coredata.dc:title: Investigation of time domain measurement of electrochemical impedance spectrum in low frequency range for lithium-ion batteries using preset equivalent circuit model
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Inui, Yoshitaka
Hirayama, Satoshi
Tanaka, Tadashi
coredata.dc:description: A time domain measurement technique using a preset equivalent circuit model consisting of many series-connected resistor and capacitor parallel elements was examined as a measurement method of the electrochemical impedance spectrum of a lithium-ion battery excluding the apparent impedances from open circuit voltage change in the low frequency range. At first, a comprehensive experimental study was carried out to determine the standard condition of the applied signal suitable for this technique. It was established that an impedance spectrum from several tens of microhertz to several tens of millihertz can be accurately measured by selecting a proper, small rate and long duration constant current charge or discharge as the applied signal. Next, impedance spectra excluding the apparent impedances from open circuit voltage change were measured under various conditions using this technique, and basic characteristics of the impedances of the solid state diffusion processes of lithium that exist in the corresponding low frequency range were investigated. It was revealed that the impedance spectrum excluding the apparent impedances from open circuit voltage change in the low frequency range, from several tens of microhertz to several tens of millihertz, can be reasonably separated into two finite length Warburg impedances that can be clearly characterized by difference in their diffusion time constant values and in the state of charge dependence of their diffusion resistances. An Arrhenius type temperature dependence was confirmed for both of their diffusion resistances.

head.ce:title: Investigation of time domain measurement of electrochemical impedance spectrum in low frequency range for lithium-ion batteries using preset equivalent circuit model

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A time domain measurement technique using a preset equivalent circuit model consisting of many series-connected resistor and capacitor parallel elements was examined as a measurement method of the electrochemical impedance spectrum of a lithium-ion battery excluding the apparent impedances from open circuit voltage change in the low frequency range. At first, a comprehensive experimental study was carried out to determine the standard condition of the applied signal suitable for this technique. It was established that an impedance spectrum from several tens of microhertz to several tens of millihertz can be accurately measured by selecting a proper, small rate and long duration constant current charge or discharge as the applied signal. Next, impedance spectra excluding the apparent impedances from open circuit voltage change were measured under various conditions using this technique, and basic characteristics of the impedances of the solid state diffusion processes of lithium that exist in the corresponding low frequency range were investigated. It was revealed that the impedance spectrum excluding the apparent impedances from open circuit voltage change in the low frequency range, from several tens of microhertz to several tens of millihertz, can be reasonably separated into two finite length Warburg impedances that can be clearly characterized by difference in their diffusion time constant values and in the state of charge dependence of their diffusion resistances. An Arrhenius type temperature dependence was confirmed for both of their diffusion resistances.

ce:section.ce:para: The use of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) is now widespread, since being introduced to portable devices, such as mobile phones and laptops, due to their advantages of light weight, high electromotive force, and high energy density. Recently, with their rapidly increasing capacity, LIBs are being used in high-power applications, such as the driving power supply of electric vehicles, smoothing batteries for solar and wind power generators, and backup batteries for mobile base stations and data centers. Among these applications, the powering of electric vehicles is one of the most important. Indeed, the promotion of electric vehicles worldwide is essential to reduce carbon dioxide emissions and protect the environment.

Manufacturers of electric vehicles are strongly requesting further efforts to increase LIB’s capacity and working life, along with the establishment of battery performance simulation technology to accurately calculate transient voltage response and heat generation of LIBs during charging and discharging. This technology is crucial to the development of high-performance LIB management systems for the safety and sophisticated control of electric vehicles.

It is well known that electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) [1] is a powerful technique that assists to solve the above problems. The performance of each electrode in an electrochemical system can be noninvasively and separately evaluated by analyzing its measured impedance spectrum data [2–5]. An equivalent circuit model (ECM) is often used to accurately calculate the transient voltage response and heat generation of an LIB [6–14]. A suitable ECM for this purpose can be derived easily through fitting calculations employing the complex nonlinear least squares method from measured impedance spectrum data [15–22]. In addition, in recent years the research and development of the state of health estimation technique of LIBs based on their ECM derived from the EIS measurement are actively conducted all over the world [23,24].

Reflecting the ease of measurement, the frequency ranges of EIS measured in almost all previous research studies fall in the middle to high frequencies between several tens of millihertz and several tens of kilohertz [1]. Concerning the relaxation frequency of each elementary process in LIBs, those of anode and cathode charge transfer processes and of anode and cathode solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) processes almost certainly exist in the above frequency range [3,5], while those of solid state diffusion processes of lithium in anode and cathode active materials are usually much lower and exist in the low frequency range from several tens of microhertz to several tens of millihertz [25].

From the relation
2 π f T = 1
between the frequency f and the corresponding characteristic response time T , the frequency of several tens of microhertz corresponds to a characteristic response time of about 1 h. This period is not so long in comparison with the typical continuous driving time of electric vehicles. During driving of electric vehicles, while charge and idle periods of LIBs are often inserted, LIBs are on a discharge trend, and so the influence of the impedance in low frequency range gradually appears with the lapse of time, because the low frequency components of the overpotential caused by this discharge trend are scarcely attenuated by the short time charge and idle periods due to their longer characteristic response times. This means that the impedance spectrum data in the above low frequency range cannot be neglected and are rather essential to estimate with high accuracy the transient voltage response and heat generation of LIBs mounted on electric vehicles. Therefore, measured impedance spectrum data of LIBs ranging from several tens of microhertz to several tens of millihertz are absolutely necessary for further improvement of LIB management systems for electric vehicles.

Frequency response analyzers (FRAs) are the most commonly used measuring instruments in EIS. They usually provide both galvanostatic electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (GEIS) and potentiostatic electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (PEIS). A sinusoidal current is applied, and the amplitude and phase shift of the output sinusoidal voltage are measured in GEIS. Conversely, a sinusoidal voltage is applied, and the current response is measured in PEIS. Since impedance is calculated individually for every frequency condition from sinusoidal current and voltage with a single frequency, the accuracy of the impedance spectrum measured by FRA is generally quite high, and so using an FRA is undoubtedly the best option for EIS in the middle to high frequency range between several tens of millihertz and several tens of kilohertz. In addition, high-specification FRAs can scan the frequency range from 10 μHz to over 1 MHz. Therefore, FRAs appear to be all-purpose devices for EIS of LIBs. However, FRA measurement of the impedance spectrum in the low frequency region is slow and impractical even for simple measurements with minimum accuracy required. For example, it takes 134 h to conduct a frequency scan from 20 μHz to 20 kHz when the selected measurement parameters are 10 measuring points per frequency decade and two duration cycles (one for delay and one for integration) for each frequency condition. In addition to this laborious measurement, the lengthy duration increases the likelihood of the linearity, stability, and causality of battery response not being maintained throughout. The 134-hour duration does not include the measurement preparation time. Regardless of the EIS method, the same amount of preparation time is required to adjust the battery’s state of charge (SOC) and temperature condition and to wait for equilibrium to be reached.

In principle, an arbitrary periodic wave can be used instead of a single sinusoidal wave as the applied signal of current or voltage in EIS, since the LIB’s voltage response for a small current application can be reasonably regarded as linear and any periodic wave can be developed in an infinite series of sinusoidal waves through a Fourier series expansion. Hence, the use of square, triangular, and multi-sine waves in GEIS and PEIS has been proposed in numerous studies, and sufficient accuracy of the measurement results has been reported [1,26–28]. By using this Fourier series expansion method, the impedances of LIB under fundamental and higher harmonic frequency conditions can be measured simultaneously without carrying out a frequency scan. This contributes to the shortening of the measurement time. However, there are many frequency conditions that should be measured, but which are not equal to an integer multiple of the fundamental frequency, especially between the fundamental and second harmonic frequencies. Individual measurements must be made under such frequency conditions. Therefore, this method is not effective in shortening the measurement time.

An arbitrary periodic current or voltage signal with a periodic response is not the only choice for EIS. An arbitrary shaped current or voltage signal, such as a rectangular pulse, Gaussian pulse, sinc pulse, step function, pseudo-random binary sequence, or broadband perturbation, and its transient response can be used [1]. The use of a periodic signal is the technique explained above, and the use of an aperiodic signal is the time domain measurement (TDM) technique. In typical TDM techniques, both the applied current or voltage signal and its measured transient response are usually Laplace or Fourier transformed, and the impedance spectrum of the LIB is calculated from the transformation results.

The convergence property of the Laplace transformation is more favorable than that of the Fourier transformation. The Laplace transformation
F ( s )
for any given time domain signal or response
f ( t )
usually converges by properly selecting the real part α of the complex number frequency parameter s . Therefore, it has been considered suitable for TDM using a single-shot current or voltage signal, and the results of many applications have been reported [29–32]. For example, a voltage step followed by a constant voltage (CV) and a square current pulse were proposed as the applied signal, and good results were presented in [29] and [30], respectively. However, in this method, α should be small and the measuring time should be long in order to obtain a sufficiently accurate impedance spectrum. A measuring time of 10 times the inverse of the minimum measuring frequency was selected in [30]. This required 139 h to measure the impedance spectrum down to 20 μHz, indicating no time-shortening effect compared with measurement by FRA.

As for EIS using the Fourier transformation, it usually uses a pseudo-random binary sequence current or broadband perturbation voltage, and is intended to measure the impedance spectrum in the middle to high frequency range [33–35]. In contrast, some research studies used a single-shot current or voltage signal and measured the impedance spectrum of the low frequency range [25,36,37]. Among them, [25] shortened the measurement time to four times the inverse of the minimum measuring frequency by optimizing the window function used in the Fourier transformation. Nevertheless, it took 56 h to measure the impedance spectrum down to 20 μHz and this is still considered as a fairly long duration.

In the measured impedance spectrum of an LIB obtained by the above outlined EIS methods, the apparent impedances from open circuit voltage (OCV) change induced by charge/discharge current flow are also included in addition to the impedances of conduction, SEI, charge transfer, and diffusion processes in the battery. Two kinds of electrochemical phenomena account for this OCV change: the charging and discharging of differential intercalation capacity [38]; and the effect of OCV hysteresis [39,40]. The former is due to the dependency of OCV on SOC and works as an apparent capacitance. This is capacitive, and so its impedance rapidly becomes large as frequency becomes low, occupying a considerable part in the imaginary part of cell impedance in a sub-millihertz condition. The OCV of an LIB draws a hysteresis loop for a low frequency alternating charge/discharge current application. This phenomenon not only works as apparent capacitance but also generates an energy loss in proportion to the area surrounded by the hysteresis loop, and so its impedance occupies a non-negligible part in both the real and imaginary parts of cell impedance in a sub-millihertz condition.

The inclusion of these apparent impedances from OCV change is inevitable for conventional EIS methods. However, they are needed in neither the electrode performance evaluation nor the overpotential estimation of LIBs. In addition, in a sub-millihertz condition, they make accurate measurement of diffusion impedance difficult. Therefore, it is strongly requested to establish an EIS method that can measure the impedance spectrum of an LIB while excluding these apparent impedances from OCV change.

A pulse-fitting method, proposed by Schmidt and Ivers-Tiffée [38] as a candidate EIS method suitable for the low frequency range, satisfies the two requirements above: measurement duration, and the exclusion of the apparent impedances from OCV change. A novel TDM approach was employed in this method. An equivalent circuit consisting of many series-connected resistor and capacitor (RC) parallel elements was adopted as the preset ECM of an LIB. Values of all its circuit constants were identified in the time domain by fitting its transient voltage response after a pulse charge or discharge current was cut off to the corresponding measurement result. The measurement duration of this method is about the same as the inverse of the minimum measuring frequency, so it takes less than 20 h to measure the impedance spectrum down to 20 μHz. In addition, this method uses only the transient voltage response under a zero current condition after the pulse current cutoff for the fitting calculation. This realizes impedance spectrum measurement of an LIB while excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change because no current flows through the LIB and its OCV is constant under this condition. The differential intercalation capacity is not obtained by the fitting calculation. However, it is rarely needed to be measured and, even if it is needed, can be separately determined from the difference in steady state cell voltages before and after the pulse current application. In the case of the Fourier transformation method, the rate and duration of the applied current pulse should be of the order of 1C and 1 min, respectively, where the C-rate is a measure of battery current and 1C is the current that fully discharges the battery in 1 h. A relatively short pulse duration is necessary to widen the measurable frequency range up to several tens of millihertz. The relatively high current rate is selected in connection with the short duration, since a large electric charge should be supplied to the LIB to excite its slow transient response. In the pulse-fitting method, the same measurement conditions as employed in the Fourier transformation method explained above were also selected. Again, a relatively high-rate and short-duration current pulse was used to induce a transient voltage response. However, the transient voltage response of an LIB often becomes nonlinear for an applied current of the order of 1C. There is a high probability that the measurement results will be insufficiently accurate, indicating that some improvement measures are required. In addition, there is a concern that an applied current pulse duration of 1 min is too short to excite a significant amount of the LIB’s slow transient response of about 10 h.

More recently, the results of impedance spectrum measurements of LIBs by the same TDM technique using a preset ECM consisting of many series-connected RC parallel elements were reported by Goldammer and Kowal [41]. Their applied current signal was a constant current (CC) charge, and the typical rate and duration were 0.5C and 0.4 h, respectively. Regarding the duration of the applied current, a little thought will tell you that the maximum measurable frequency is not restricted by the duration of the applied current when measurement data are used to identify the values of the circuit constants of the preset ECM in the time domain. The long-duration CC charge itself is quite acceptable as an applied signal, but it should be stated that the condition of the applied charge current (0.5C and 0.4 h) is irrelevant, as 20 % of the total battery capacity is charged during this CC charge. This is obviously too large. While the applied signal current of an LIB inevitably causes SOC change, it should be as small as possible. This is because SOC variation in an LIB is necessarily accompanied by variation of its electrochemical impedance, and so is in conflict with the assumption of the employed TDM technique that the circuit constants of the ECM are constant during measurement. In addition, in their report the lower limit frequency of the measured impedance was selected as 0.5 mHz and this frequency is obviously too high to make clear the impedances of solid state diffusion processes of lithium in anode and cathode active materials.

The above observation indicates that, although the TDM technique using a preset ECM consisting of many series-connected RC parallel elements is presently the most suitable method of measuring the electrochemical impedance spectrum of an LIB while excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change in the low frequency range, the conditions of the applied current signals used in the literature were unsuitable. A small-rate and long-duration CC charge or discharge should be selected, instead of the reported values, to satisfy the requirements of a linear response and a small SOC change in the LIB simultaneously.

Considering these backgrounds, in this study, the authors concentrated on this TDM technique using a preset ECM, and at first experimentally investigated appropriate condition of the applied small-rate, long-duration CC charge or discharge signal required to accurately measure the electrochemical impedance spectrum of an LIB while excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change in the low frequency range from several tens of microhertz to several tens of millihertz. For this purpose, a commercially available cylindrical 18,650 type LIB was employed as the test battery. Measurements were carried out under various signal conditions, and the results obtained were comprehensively examined and compared. The authors then measured impedance spectra of the test battery while excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change under various SOC and battery temperature conditions and clarified basic characteristics of impedances of the solid state diffusion processes of lithium in anode and cathode active materials in the corresponding low frequency range.

As the most appropriate method to accurately measure the electrochemical impedance spectrum of an LIB while excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change in the low frequency range from several tens of microhertz to several tens of millihertz, this study focused on a TDM technique using a preset ECM consisting of many series-connected RC parallel elements. The reasons for choosing and the suitability of this type of ECM are first explained in this section. The principle and fitting procedure of the TDM technique are then briefly introduced.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECM of LIB in the low frequency range
ce:section.ce:para: For EIS of an LIB in the middle to high frequency range from several tens of millihertz to several tens of kilohertz, it is widely accepted that the use of an FRA is the best option due to its adequate accuracy and short measuring time. Hence, in this work the measurement frequency range of the selected TDM technique using a preset ECM was set as the low frequency range from several tens of microhertz to several tens of millihertz.

This series expansion demonstrates that a finite length Warburg impedance can be replaced with an infinite equivalent circuit consisting of series-connected RC parallel elements with resistance

More generally, according to the theoretical base of the distribution of relaxation analysis [4,36,37], except for the high frequency range an impedance of an arbitrary electrochemical system excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change can be expressed as the following Z using an integral equation as a function of the complex number frequency parameter s :

Therefore, the infinite equivalent circuit consisting of series-connected RC parallel elements has a wider application range by properly selecting the time constant of each RC parallel element, since it is an equivalent circuit of the second term on the right side of Eq. (4).

Considering this, the present work employed an equivalent circuit consisting of series-connected

This makes accurate impedance measurement possible as the equivalent circuit shown in Fig. 3 is applicable even in the case the impedance of the diffusion process cannot be exactly formulated by the finite length Warburg impedance

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement procedure of the TDM technique using preset ECM
ce:section.ce:para: As the applied signal of the TDM technique using a preset ECM, both the CC discharge and charge of small rate and long duration can be used. In this subsection, applying a CC discharge signal is taken as an example, and this TDM’s measurement procedure is explained. When applying a CC charge, measurement can be carried out in a similar manner, although its explanation is omitted.

Fig. 4 shows typical time variations of cell voltage and current during measurement. In this figure, the time origin
t = 0
is selected as the moment when the applied small rate and long duration CC discharge, of magnitude
I 0
and duration time
T 0
, is cut off. It follows that the start time of the CC discharge is at
t = -
T 0
. Therefore, it is necessary for the test battery to have reached equilibrium by
t = -
T 0
under an appropriate battery temperature and SOC condition. The temperature is kept constant throughout the measurement. The SOC should be the value of its measurement condition at
t = 0
, and so must be regulated at
t = -
T 0
considering the slight but non-zero SOC change caused by the applied CC discharge.

As for acquiring voltage data, the cell voltage
V c
( t )
is first monitored and recorded. It is started immediately before the cutoff of the applied CC discharge and is continued in every prescribed, sufficiently short time interval. In the present TDM method, the recovery voltage
V r
( t )
( t > 0 )

The unknown circuit constants to be identified in the present TDM technique are the values of the resistances
R n
( n = 0 ,

. Their most probable values can in principle be obtained by solving the minimization problem of the above sum of squared residuals J using the least squares method. However, there are two difficulties in this fitting calculation. One is the positivity of resistances. The values of the resistances
R n
should be greater than or equal to zero. This constraint can be satisfied by employing a non-negative least squares algorithm. The other is the ill-posed nature of the minimization problem. To overcome this difficulty, the commonly used Tikhonov regularization is applied. Specifically, a regularization term is added to the sum of squared residuals J and Eq. (8) is modified as follows:
(12)

are identified by solving the minimization problem of the modified sum of squared residuals J shown in Eq. (12) using the non-negative least squares method. Since the ECM shown in Fig. 3 is employed, the impedance spectrum of an LIB excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change in the low frequency range, which is the measurement target of the present TDM technique, can then be calculated by substituting the identified resistance values into the following equation:
(14)

where ω is the angular frequency and j is the imaginary unit. While the above fitting calculation cannot determine the apparent impedances from OCV change as same as that of the previous TDM methods [38,41], this is not a problem as the apparent impedances are rarely needed to be measured.

The authors first disassembled a commercially available battery pack and extracted a cylindrical 18,650 type LIB: a Sony US18650GR with 18 mm diameter and 65 mm length. It had a nominal voltage of 3.6 V and a nominal battery capacity of 2.2 Ah. The battery’s cathode and anode materials were lithium nickel-manganese-cobalt oxide and graphite, respectively. Since the impedance spectrum of this extracted battery in its unused state was not steady and changed rapidly with successive charge/discharge cycles, the authors next deteriorated it through 150 repetitions of a full charge/discharge cycle, and then used it as a test battery. With regard to the condition and procedure of this deterioration process, the battery temperature was kept at 20 °C, and a CC/CV charging (2.2 A to 4.2 V and 3 h at 4.2 V) followed by a CC discharging (2.2 A to 2.7 V) was selected as the charge/discharge cycle. The battery’s capacity was reduced to about 2.0 Ah by this treatment, so the authors adopted 2.0 Ah as the reference value for determining the SOC and C-rate in the measurement experiments.

The impedance of an LIB is known to become large and its characteristic to be clear as its temperature becomes low, indicating that a low battery temperature is advantageous for an appropriate ECM decision and circuit constant identification [18]. Hence, the measurement experiments were mainly conducted at a low battery temperature of − 10 °C. No essential defect exists in adopting this low temperature condition instead of room temperature, since Arrhenius-type temperature dependence generally holds for the resistance of each elementary process in an LIB between room temperature and low temperature conditions [43].

After the above pretreatment and before proceeding to the main experiments, the full CC discharge characteristic at 10 mA (0.005C) and full CC/CV charge characteristic at 10 mA/4.2 V were measured at a battery temperature of − 10 °C in order to grasp the approximate OCV of the test battery for every SOC condition. The obtained discharge and charge characteristic curves are shown in Fig. 5 . Although the OCV is not uniquely determined by the SOC due to the effect of OCV hysteresis [39,40], it is certain that the OCV takes a value between these two characteristic curves.

In all experiments, the charge/discharge current of the test battery was supplied from a battery test system Kikusui PFX2000, battery voltage was measured using a digital multimeter Agilent 34410A, and battery temperature control was performed in an environmental test chamber Etac SLN401.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of impedance spectrum in the middle to high frequency range by FRA
ce:section.ce:para: Although the research target is to measure the impedance spectrum of an LIB while excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change in the low frequency range by employing a TDM technique using a preset ECM, impedance data in the middle to high frequency range are also needed to confirm continuity towards the middle frequency range and consistency of the measured impedance spectrum data in the low frequency range. Hence, prior to the TDM, the impedance spectra of the test battery in the middle to high frequency range were measured using a Solartron 1280C FRA.

First, the measurements were made at a battery temperature of − 10 °C and SOC of 50 %. The scanned frequency range was set at 5 mHz to 20 kHz, and the selected measurement interval was 10 points per frequency decade. The PEIS mode was used. Measurements were carried out in two cases, with amplitudes of applied voltage perturbation of 15 and 25 mV. The Nyquist plots of the obtained impedance spectra are shown in Fig. 6 . The complete coincidence of the loci for both 15 and 25 mV can be confirmed from this figure, and so the voltage amplitude was fixed at 25 mV in the subsequent measurements using the FRA.

Then, to confirm continuity towards the middle frequency range in various SOC and battery temperature conditions, the impedance spectrum in the frequency range of 5 mHz to 20 kHz was measured by the FRA under various SOC conditions for each of five battery temperature conditions: −10, 0, 10, 20, and 30 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of impedance in the low frequency range by square wave current
ce:section.ce:para: In the measurement of the impedance spectra of LIBs in the low frequency range, conventional EIS methods are slow and impractical. In addition, as for their results, the apparent impedances from OCV change are mixed with the impedances of the diffusion processes. Nevertheless, impedance measurement in the low frequency range using conventional EIS methods is helpful in demonstrating the undesirable effect of these apparent impedances.

Therefore, impedances in the sub-millihertz frequency range were measured for the test battery using a conventional EIS method at a battery temperature of − 10 °C and SOC of 50 %. Since the FRA employed could not scan the sub-millihertz frequency range, Fourier series expansion method was selected as the EIS method. Specifically, three cycles of square wave alternative currents with an amplitude of ± 4 mA and frequencies of 10, 20, and 200 μHz were applied while keeping an average SOC of 50 %, and the voltage responses were measured. The capacity of the test battery was 2.0 Ah, and so the electric charges charged and discharged during one cycle were 2.8 %, 1.4 %, and 0.14 % of the total battery capacity for the 10, 20, and 200 μHz square waves, respectively. The fundamental and odd-times higher harmonic wave components were calculated for the current and voltage waveforms of the last cycle, and the impedance at each frequency was determined from them.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of impedance in the low frequency range by the TDM technique
ce:section.ce:para: The experiments proceeded to measurements for the main research purpose. The impedance spectra of the test battery excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change in the low frequency range were measured by the chosen TDM technique using a preset ECM.

First, a small rate and long duration CC discharge of a magnitude of 10 mA and duration of 4 h was selected as the standard condition of the applied signal, and TDM was performed on the test battery at a battery temperature of − 10 °C and SOC of 50 %. Since its capacity was 2.0 Ah, the current rate of the applied signal was 0.005C and the electric charge discharged was 2.0 % of the total battery capacity. This SOC change is non-negligible but was judged to be safely admissible. It is quite small, and an LIB’s impedance cannot be measured without being accompanied by charge or discharge current flow. After the applied current signal was cut off, the battery voltage was monitored and recorded for 50000 s (about 14 h). Equal intervals on a logarithmic time scale with 100 points per time decade were set for both series of the time points of recovery voltage measurement
t m
( m = 1 ,
2 , ⋯ ,
M )
and the preset time constants of RC parallel elements
τ n
( n = 1 ,
2 , ⋯ ,
N )
, and
M = 501
,
t 1
= 5.0 ×
10
- 1
s
,
t M
= 5.0 ×
10
4
s
,
N = 701
,
τ 1
= 5.0 ×
10
- 2
s
, and
τ N
= 5.0 ×
10
5
s
were selected for the present TDM. The minimization problem of the sum of squared residuals J shown in Eq. (12) was solved by using a non-negative least squares method included in the MATLAB numeric computing environment. As for the dimensionless regularization parameter λ for solving the minimization problem, its default value was set at 10.0. The suitability of this default λ was confirmed through comprehensive comparison between the fitting results in the cases λ was set at 10.0, 30.0, and 50.0. In addition, measurements of the test battery were carried out under differing signal conditions shown in Table 1 to confirm the suitability of the standard signal condition.

Following the above experiments for signal condition setting and adequacy verification, the impedance spectra of the test battery excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change in the low frequency range were measured under various battery conditions to clarify the SOC and battery temperature dependence of the impedances of the solid state diffusion processes of lithium in anode and cathode active materials. Battery temperature was first set at − 10 °C and the TDM was carried out under a total of 46 SOC conditions, from 98 % to 8 % at 2 % intervals. In addition, the battery temperature was changed sequentially from − 10 °C to 0, 10, 20, and then 30 °C, and the TDM was performed under three SOC conditions of 80 %, 50 %, and 20 % at each temperature. Next, the impedance spectra obtained in the low frequency range were separated into the impedances of two diffusion processes, and their characteristics were investigated individually. This separation was carried out by fitting an ECM that included two series-connected finite length Warburg impedances to each measured spectrum. A program using a complex nonlinear least squares method, included in the ZPlot general purpose electrochemical impedance software package, was employed for this fitting calculation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Frequency range providing reliable impedance value
ce:section.ce:para: At first, the full time sequence data of the recovery voltage up to 50000 s were measured for the test battery at a battery temperature of − 10 °C and SOC of 50 %. In this experiment, the standard condition of CC discharge with a magnitude of 10 mA and duration of 4 h was selected as the applied signal, and the dimensionless regularization parameter λ for solving the minimization problem was set as its default value of 10.0. The validity confirmation of this signal condition and the value of λ will be discussed in the subsequent subsections.

In the employed TDM technique, a low frequency range providing reliable impedance values is determined in relation to the end time of the time sequence data, since the longer time variation data are needed in order to obtain the lower frequency components. In the measurements, this end time was selected as 50000 s, ensuring all transient responses having a characteristic response time shorter than about 8000 s are completely attenuated and extinguished before this time point as 50000 s is 6.25 times longer than 8000 s and
e
- 6.25
= 1.9 ×
10
- 3
≅ 0 .
From the relation
2 π f T = 1
between the frequency f and the corresponding characteristic response time T , the characteristic response time of 8000 s corresponds to the frequency of 20 μHz. Therefore, impedance spectrum components higher than 20 μHz scarcely affect time variation of the recovery voltage after
t = 50000
s
, naturally allowing impedance spectrum components higher than 20 μHz to be determined only using the time variation data of the recovery voltage before
t = 50000
s
. Similarly, the impedance spectrum components higher than 200 μHz and 2 mHz can be determined by only using the time variation data of the recovery voltage before
t = 5000
and
500
s
, respectively.

Based on this theoretical knowledge, at first, the TDM technique was applied to the entire above measured time sequence data up to 50000 s and the impedance spectrum excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change was calculated down to 20 μHz. In addition, the TDM technique was also applied and the impedance spectra down to 200 μHz and 2 mHz were calculated by using the first parts of the same data up to 5000 s (
M = 401
,
t 1
= 5.0 ×
10
- 1
s
,
t M
= 5.0 ×
10
3
s
) and 500 s (
M = 301
,
t 1
= 5.0 ×
10
- 1
s
,
t M
= 5.0 ×
10
2
s
), respectively. As the lower limit frequency of the FRA measurement was set at 5 mHz, the impedance spectra were calculated up to 20 mHz in all of the three TDM cases to secure an appropriate overlap frequency range with the FRA result. The Nyquist plots and the frequency characteristics of the real and imaginary parts of the impedance spectra excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change obtained by these TDM calculations are shown in Fig. 7 . The measurement result of the impedance spectrum in the frequency range from 5 mHz to 20 kHz obtained by the FRA under the same battery condition (for which the Nyquist plot was already shown in Fig. 6) is also depicted in this figure.

Regarding the Nyquist plot loci and the frequency characteristic curves obtained by the executed three kinds of TDM calculations, it can be seen from Fig. 7 that neither of the results using the first parts of the measured time sequence data up to 5000 and 500 s deviates at all from those using the whole data up to 50000 s. This is a natural consequence of the above theoretical knowledge and does not contradict it, validating the calculation algorithm of the employed TDM technique.

On the other hand, as shown in Fig. 7, in the frequency range between 5 and 20 mHz, the Nyquist plot and frequency characteristics obtained by the TDM technique completely coincide with those directly measured by the FRA. This frequency range corresponds to a relaxation time of about 10–30 s, which is insufficient for non-negligible variation to occur in the level of OCV hysteresis. The effects of differential intercalation capacity can be ignored in the frequency region higher than 5 mHz. Therefore, the observed coincidence between the TDM and FRA results indicates continuity and consistency of the TDM result towards the middle frequency range, and so validates the measured time sequence data used in the TDM calculations. Synthesizing these results, it can be concluded that the employed TDM technique using a preset ECM is reliable as an impedance measurement method in the frequency range from 20 μHz to 20 mHz for an LIB.

Concerning the quantitative accuracy of the above shown impedance spectrum excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change obtained by the TDM technique, multifaceted discussion and confirmation will be provided in the subsequent subsections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Verification of appropriateness of the default dimensionless regularization parameter
ce:section.ce:para: As for the dimensionless regularization parameter λ in the Tikhonov regularization term for solving the minimization problem of the modified sum of squared residuals J shown in Eq. (12), the default value was set at 10.0 and used in the TDM calculation discussed in the preceding subsection. To confirm the appropriateness of this choice, the minimization problem was solved in three cases where λ was set at 10.0 (default value), 30.0, and 50.0, using the same measurement data obtained under the standard signal condition at a battery temperature of − 10 °C and SOC of 50 %. Since the time variation of the recovery voltage after the applied CC discharge cutoff can be reconstructed from the identified ECM, the reconstructed time variations were calculated in these three λ cases and compared with the corresponding original measurement data.

The comparison results in the full time range from 0.5 to 50000 s and magnifications from 0.5 to 2500 s and from 0.5 to 125 s are depicted in Fig. 8 . In this figure, the ordinate represents the apparent internal resistance, defined as a quotient of the recovery voltage divided by the magnitude of the CC signal, since the unknown circuit constants to be identified through fitting calculation are the values of the resistances in the ECM. In addition, absolute values of relative error at all measured points were calculated and averaged in the three λ cases. They were 0.208 %, 0.575 %, and 0.777 % for λ being equal to 10.0 (the default value), 30.0, and 50.0, respectively. It can be confirmed from Fig. 8 that, while the fitting results deviate slightly from the measured result when λ is set at 30.0 and 50.0, the deviation becomes negligible and both coincidence and smoothness are excellent when λ is 10.0. Correspondingly, the relative error decreases with reduction of λ and is sufficiently small when λ is 10.0. From these comparison results, it can be safely judged that the default dimensionless regularization parameter of 10.0 is appropriate.

Furthermore, the very good coincidence seen above between the reconstructed and measured results in the default dimensionless regularization parameter case also indicates that the identified ECM has sufficient validity and consistency with the measured time sequence data of the recovery voltage. This fact is a reliable evidence of the quantitative accuracy of the measured impedance spectrum excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change already shown in Fig. 7, as the same reconstructed voltage response can be obtained from both the identified ECM and its corresponding impedance spectrum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Verification of appropriateness of standard signal condition
ce:section.ce:para: It was explained that, in the TDM experiment, the standard condition of CC discharge with a magnitude of 10 mA and duration of 4 h was selected for the applied signal for the measurement. To confirm the appropriateness of this standard condition, TDM experiments were carried out for various signal cases at a battery temperature of − 10 °C and SOC of 50 %. The impedance spectra obtained were compared with each other.

First, to confirm the sufficiency of the standard signal condition, several experiments under adjacent conditions were also performed. Fig. 9 shows Nyquist plots of experimentally measured impedance spectra excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change in applied signal conditions: CC discharge with a magnitude of 10 mA and duration of 4 h (the standard condition); CC charge with a magnitude of 10 mA and duration of 4 h; CC discharge with a magnitude of 5 mA and duration of 4 h; and CC charge with a magnitude of 5 mA and duration of 4 h. In this figure, each locus of the TDM result is connected to that of the corresponding FRA result at 10 mHz (this display method is also used in the following Nyquist plots). All spectra shown in Fig. 9 agree well with each other. This indicates linearity of voltage response to applied current when the current is under 10 mA for both discharge and charge. Thus, the current direction and magnitude of the standard condition (discharge, 10 mA) can be safely judged as valid. On the other hand, Fig. 10 shows Nyquist plots of experimentally measured impedance spectra excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change in applied signal conditions: CC discharge with a magnitude of 10 mA and duration of 4 h (the standard condition); CC charge with a magnitude of 10 mA and duration of 4 h; CC discharge with a magnitude of 10 mA and duration of 3 h; and CC charge with a magnitude of 10 mA and duration of 3 h. This figure indicates that the obtained results are almost the same whether the duration is 4 or 3 h. Since a longer duration is certainly better for obtaining reliable impedance spectra in the low frequency range, the current duration of the standard condition (4 h) can be safely judged as valid from this comparison. From the above observations, the sufficiency of the current magnitude and duration of the standard signal condition was confirmed.

Next, to confirm the necessity of the standard signal condition, several additional experiments were performed with larger current magnitudes or shorter current durations. Fig. 11 shows Nyquist plots of experimentally measured impedance spectra excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change in applied signal conditions: CC discharge with a magnitude of 10 mA and duration of 4 h (the standard condition); CC discharge with a magnitude of 20 mA and duration of 4 h; and CC discharge with a magnitude of 40 mA and duration of 4 h. This figure indicates that impedance decreases as the current magnitude becomes large. This is caused by the effect of nonlinearity between the current and voltage. This nonlinearity can no longer be ignored when the current magnitude is 20 mA or larger, demonstrating the necessity of the current magnitude of the standard condition (10 mA). Fig. 12 shows Nyquist plots of experimentally measured impedance spectra excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change in applied signal conditions: CC discharge with a magnitude of 10 mA and duration of 4 h (the standard condition); CC discharge with a magnitude of 10 mA and duration of 30 min; and CC discharge with a magnitude of 10 mA and duration of 10 min. This figure indicates that the obtained impedances are remarkably small when the durations of the applied current signal are 30 and 10 min. Consequently, an applied current duration of less than several tens of minutes is evidently too short. A duration of a few hours is proven to be necessary to excite a significant amount of slow transient voltage response corresponding to several tens to hundreds of microhertz.

The above multifaceted experiments indicate the necessity and sufficiency of the applied signal condition of a CC discharge with a magnitude of 10 mA and duration of 4 h, confirming the appropriateness of the standard condition. In addition, these examination results clearly reveal the inaccuracy of the measurement results of the previous study reported by Schmidt and Ivers-Tiffée [38] as their selected applied signal condition of current pulse with 1C (which corresponds to 2 A in the case of the test battery) and 1 min order is obviously deviated largely from the above described its necessary condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison with impedances measured using square wave current
ce:section.ce:para: Under the condition of a battery temperature of − 10 °C and SOC of 50 %, the impedances of the test battery were measured not only by FRA and the TDM technique using a preset ECM as shown in Fig. 7, but also by the Fourier series expansion method using square wave alternative currents with an amplitude of ± 4 mA and frequencies of 10, 20, and 200 μHz as applied signals. The Nyquist plots of the impedance spectra obtained by these three methods are summarized in Fig. 13 . In the case of a square wave alternative current, its Fourier series expansion contains the fundamental and odd-times higher harmonic wave components. Making good use of this property, for the measured impedance data of the Fourier series expansion method, 15 frequency conditions from 20 μHz to 4.2 mHz arranged at approximately equal intervals on a logarithmic frequency axis were selected among the fundamental and odd-times higher harmonic frequencies of 10, 20, and 200 μHz, which are the frequencies of the applied square wave alternative currents. Impedances at these frequency conditions are calculated and plotted in this figure. Specifically, the impedances of 30, 50, 70, and 130 μHz are calculated from the 10 μHz data; those of 20, 100, 300, and 420 μHz from the 20 μHz data; and those of 200 and 600 μHz, and 1.0, 1.4, 2.2, 3.0, and 4.2 mHz from the 200 μHz data. All measured impedances obtained by the Fourier series expansion method line up smoothly even though various harmonic components of three kinds of voltage responses for square wave alternative currents with different frequencies are independently used in their calculations. In addition, the continuity and consistency of this measurement point sequence towards the FRA result are fairly good. Therefore, the impedance spectrum measured by the Fourier series expansion method can be judged valid and reliable as a result of the conventional EIS method which includes the apparent impedances from OCV change.

It can be seen from Fig. 13 that there is a large discrepancy between the impedance spectra measured by the TDM technique and the Fourier series expansion method. Regarding both the real and imaginary parts of the impedance spectrum in the low frequency range, the values measured by the Fourier series expansion method are generally appreciably larger than the corresponding values measured by the TDM technique using a preset ECM. The discrepancy of the imaginary part becomes notably large under the sub-millihertz condition, since the impedance measured by the Fourier series expansion method steeply grows in the direction of the imaginary axis as the frequency becomes low.

To quantitatively examine the above large discrepancy, Fig. 14 shows the periodic voltage responses against the square wave alternative currents with an amplitude of ± 4 mA and frequencies of 10, 20, and 200 μHz under the − 10 °C battery temperature condition. The abscissa of this figure was selected as the SOC to clearly reveal the OCV variation attributable to the SOC change by applied square wave alternative current. The voltage variations around an SOC of 50 % extracted from the full CC discharge characteristic at 10 mA and the full CC/CV charge characteristic at 10 mA/4.2 V already shown in Fig. 5 are also depicted in Fig. 14.

In the case of the Fourier series expansion method, from the periodic voltage responses against the square wave alternative currents at frequencies of 10 and 20 μHz shown in Fig. 14 it can be reasonably estimated that the OCV of the test battery increases or decreases by about 7 mV when 1.0 % of the total battery capacity is charged or discharged under an SOC of around 50 %. Similarly, the OCV difference of about 4 mV between the two conditions of an SOC of 49.5 % and SOC of 50.5 % can be reasonably estimated from the characteristic curves of CC discharge with 10 mA and CC/CV charge with 10 mA/4.2 V depicted in Fig. 14. In the latter case, OCV hysteresis is completely saturated by the long-term discharge or charge, and so this OCV change can reasonably be ascribed to the differential intercalation capacity. Therefore, the difference in these OCV changes of about 3 mV is attributed to the effect of OCV hysteresis, and this reveals that in the low frequency range the apparent impedances from OCV change, due not only to the differential intercalation capacity but also to the non-negligible effect of OCV hysteresis, are mixed with the impedances of the diffusion processes in the impedance spectrum measured by conventional EIS methods.

As the test battery’s capacity was 2.0 Ah, the above OCV variation against the square wave alternative currents at frequencies of 10 and 20 μHz indicates that the apparent capacitance from OCV change is about 1.0 × 104F, and the magnitude of its impedance is about 0.80, 0.53, 0.32, and 0.16 Ω at frequencies of 20, 30, 50, and 100 μHz, respectively. In theory, the difference in the imaginary part of the measured impedance spectra between the TDM technique and the Fourier series expansion method should quantitatively coincide with the impedance of the apparent capacitance from OCV change included in the measurement results by the Fourier series expansion method, as the TDM technique measures the impedance spectrum while excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change. As can be seen from Fig. 13, the values of the imaginary part of the cell impedance measured by the TDM technique and the Fourier series expansion method are − 0.08 and − 0.89 Ω, respectively, at 20 μHz, −0.08 and − 0.61 Ω, respectively, at 30 μHz, −0.08 and − 0.41 Ω, respectively, at 50 μHz, and − 0.08 and − 0.26 Ω, respectively, at 100 μHz. Therefore, the difference in the imaginary part between the impedances measured by the two methods is 0.81, 0.53, 0.33, and 0.18 Ω at 20, 30, 50, and 100 μHz, respectively, coinciding very well in all of the four frequency conditions with the above described impedance of the apparent capacitance and so clearly indicating the quantitative accuracy of the imaginary part of the impedance spectrum excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change measured by the TDM technique using a preset ECM.

On the other hand, in the low frequency range the real part of the cell impedance excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change at the frequency f can be estimated with reasonably good accuracy as the apparent internal resistance (the quotient of the recovery voltage divided by the magnitude of the CC signal) at
t = T = 1 / ( 2 π f )
, where T is the characteristic response time corresponding to the frequency f . This estimation is based on the following simplification reasonable in the low frequency range for the equivalent circuit shown in Fig. 3. For both purposes of approximately obtaining the alternative current response at the frequency f and the recovery voltage at
t = T
after the CC signal cutoff, a same series circuit comprising the OCV
V
o c
, resistance
R 0
, and one resistance with the sum total value of the resistances in the series-connected RC parallel elements having a time constant shorter than the characteristic response time T can be used as the simplified circuit of Fig. 3. Therefore, the approximate value of the real part of the cell impedance excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change at frequencies of 20, 30, 50, and 100 μHz can be obtained from the time variation of the apparent internal resistance already shown in Fig. 8 as 0.81, 0.79, 0.77, and 0.74 Ω, respectively, since these four frequencies correspond to the characteristic response times of 8000, 5300, 3200, and 1600 s, respectively. In connection with this, as can be seen from Fig. 13, the value of the real part of the cell impedance measured by the TDM technique is 0.82, 0.79, 0.76, and 0.73 Ω at 20, 30, 50, and 100 μHz, respectively, and coincides very well in all of the four frequency conditions with the corresponding approximate value described above. This result clearly indicates the quantitative accuracy of the real part of the impedance spectrum excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change measured by the TDM technique using a preset ECM. In contrast, as shown in Fig. 13, the value of the real part of the cell impedance measured by the Fourier series expansion method is 0.94, 0.88, 0.82, and 0.78 Ω at 20, 30, 50, and 100 μHz, respectively. The discrepancy from the corresponding approximate value becomes appreciable as the frequency decreases, and the 0.94 Ω at 20 μHz is obviously too large since the apparent internal resistance shown in Fig. 8 does not exceed 0.85 Ω even at
t =
50000 s, revealing that in the low frequency range not only the remarkable apparent capacitance from OCV change but also this non-negligible apparent resistance from OCV hysteresis are included as the obstructive apparent impedances in the impedance spectrum measured by conventional EIS methods.

The comprehensive examination described above clearly demonstrates the capability and quantitative accuracy of the TDM technique to measure both the real and imaginary parts of the impedance spectrum of an LIB while excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change that make accurate measurement of diffusion impedances difficult in the low frequency range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative investigation of SOC dependence of diffusion impedances
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 15 shows examples of the measured results of the test battery under various SOC conditions, with Nyquist plots of the impedance spectra excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change in the cases of a battery temperature of − 10 °C and SOCs of 80 %, 60 %, 40 %, and 20 %. Each locus of the TDM result is connected to that of the corresponding FRA result at 10 mHz. This figure shows that the impedance spectrum largely varies depending on the SOC. It also clearly shows that there are two diffusion processes in the low frequency range. One exists on the higher frequency side between 200 μHz and 10 mHz, and the other on the lower frequency side below 200 μHz. It is worth mentioning that this clear observation is attributed to the advantage of the employed TDM technique using a preset ECM that can measure the impedance spectrum while excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change.

Based on the above visual inspection, in the present study, two diffusion processes were preset in the low frequency range below 10 mHz and were identified for each of the measured impedance spectra by using the ECM fitting technique based on a complex nonlinear least squares method. Specifically, a circuit consisting of three series-connected circuit elements of resistance
R 0
and two finite length Warburg impedances
Z
w h
and
Z
w l
simulating the higher and lower frequency side diffusion processes, respectively, comprised the ECM. Among these elements,
R 0
is the total resistance of the conduction, SEI, and charge transfer processes. As for
Z
w h
and
Z
w l
, not the ideal type but the following generalized type [44,45] was adopted to obtain more appropriate fitting results:
(15)
Z
w h
ω
=
R
d h
j ω
τ
d h
p h
tanh
j ω
τ
d h
p h
,
(16)
Z
w l
ω
=
R
d l
j ω
τ
d l
p l
tanh
j ω
τ
d l

,
where
τ
d h
and
τ
d l
are the diffusion time constants of the higher and lower frequency side finite length Warburg impedances, respectively,
R
d h
and
R
d l
are the diffusion resistances of the higher and lower frequency side finite length Warburg impedances, respectively,
p h
and
p l
are the adjustable power indexes of the higher and lower frequency side finite length Warburg impedances, respectively, which are positive and equal to or lower than 0.5.

Examples of the fitting results are shown in Fig. 16 . In this figure, the Nyquist plots of the impedance spectra of the identified ECMs and those of their original measurement data are compared in the frequency range between 20 μHz and 10 mHz in the cases of a temperature of − 10 °C and SOCs of 60 % and 40 %. Very good fitting results were obtained in both cases.

The finite length Warburg impedance, that is one of the theoretical and simplified diffusion impedance models, is correct only under a specific condition. However, the above very good coincidence between the measured and fitting results strongly supports the validity of adopting the finite length Warburg impedances in the ECM of LIBs for practical applications. In addition, the finite length Warburg impedance is a conventionally and widely accepted most convenient model for the diffusion impedance in LIBs caused by the solid state diffusion of lithium in anode and cathode active materials [4], and the basic concept of the finite length Warburg impedance is also accepted as valid and utilized even in the recently proposed and reported microscopic and more detailed diffusion impedance models for LIBs [46]. These facts and the coincidence of the circuit configuration with the ECM shown in Fig. 2 are the reason for the selection of the above ECM including the finite length Warburg impedances in the present investigation.

The large variation of battery impedance associated with the change of its SOC in the low frequency range, as shown in Fig. 15, indicates the necessity of grasping the SOC dependence of the two diffusion processes introduced above, if we are to understand battery characteristics. For this purpose, the diffusion time constants of the higher and lower frequency side finite length Warburg impedances (
τ
d h
and
τ
d l
) and the diffusion resistances of the higher and lower frequency side finite length Warburg impedances (
R
d h
and
R
d l
) were identified for the measurement results in a total of 46 conditions of a battery temperature of − 10 °C and SOCs varying from 98 % to 8 % at 2 % intervals. The obtained SOC dependence of the diffusion time constants and the diffusion resistances is summarized and depicted in Fig. 17 . The diffusion time constants are displayed using a logarithmic axis, as the employed ECM covers a wide frequency range from several tens of microhertz to several tens of millihertz.

Regarding the diffusion time constants, Fig. 17(a) shows that, while both
τ
d h
and
τ
d l
are almost constant and independent of SOC, their values greatly differ;
τ
d l
is several tens of times longer than
τ
d h
. Specifically,
τ
d h
and
τ
d l
range from several hundred to 1000 s (about 10 min) and from 10,000 to several tens of thousands of seconds (about 10 h), respectively. Therefore, it is revealed that the two diffusion processes can be distinguished from each other over the whole range of SOC by their time constants. Regarding the diffusion resistances, Fig. 17(b) shows that, while
R
d h
is almost constant independent of SOC,
R
d l
varies greatly depending on SOC and takes a local maximum value around the two SOC conditions of 30 % and 65 %. It is well known that a variety of peculiar phenomena attributable to the staging and stage transition phenomena of a graphite anode [47–49] usually occur under these SOC conditions. One example can be seen in Fig. 5, and is the unsmooth change of the gradients of CC discharge and CC/CV charge characteristics around the same SOC conditions. From these measurement results and well known facts, it is highly probable that the lower frequency side finite length Warburg impedance
Z
w l
comes from a solid state diffusion process of lithium in anode active material and is identical to the anode side finite length Warburg impedance
Z
w a
in the corresponding ECM shown in Fig. 2. In addition, this necessarily leads to the consequence that the higher frequency side finite length Warburg impedance
Z
w h
comes from the solid state diffusion process of lithium in cathode active material and is identical to the cathode side finite length Warburg impedance
Z
w c
. Although these identifications are highly probable, their confirmation requires further work, which is beyond the scope of the present study and will be addressed in the future.

The discussion in this subsection reveals that, in the low frequency range from several tens of microhertz to several tens of millihertz, the impedance spectrum of an LIB excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change can be reasonably separated into two finite length Warburg (diffusion) impedances that can be clearly characterized by the difference in their diffusion time constant values and in the SOC dependence of their diffusion resistances. Concerning this kind of separation result of elementary processes using two series-connected finite length Warburg impedances in the low frequency range from several tens of microhertz to several tens of millihertz, no previous research has reported and no information has been clarified until now. The present study is the first successful case, largely owing to the appropriate application of a fundamentally excellent TDM technique using a preset ECM which can measure the impedance spectrum while excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative investigation of the temperature dependence of diffusion impedances
ce:section.ce:para:
In the present study, the impedance spectra of the test battery excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change were measured under four battery temperature conditions (0, 10, 20, and 30 °C) in addition to − 10 °C. Changes in Nyquist plots of the obtained impedance spectra for battery temperature variations from − 10 to 10 and 30 °C in the cases of SOC 80 %, 50 %, and 20 % are illustrated in Fig. 18 . Each locus of the TDM result is connected to that of the corresponding FRA result at 10 mHz. This figure shows that the impedance of diffusion processes in the low frequency range has a large battery temperature dependence and rapidly decreases as temperature rises, just like that of SEI and charge transfer processes in the middle frequency range. To quantitatively understand this temperature dependence in the low frequency range, the measured impedances were at first separated into higher and lower frequency side finite length Warburg impedances (
Z
w h
and
Z
w l
) employing the same fitting technique explained in the preceding subsection. The temperature dependence of their identified parameters of higher and lower frequency side diffusion time constants (
τ
d h
and
τ
d l
) and higher and lower frequency side diffusion resistances (
R
d h
and
R
d l
) was then examined in detail.

The temperature dependence of the diffusion time constants of the higher frequency side and lower frequency side finite length Warburg impedances (
τ
d h
and
τ
d l
) at SOCs of 80 %, 50 %, and 20 % is summarized and shown in Fig. 19 . It was revealed that the time constants do not change greatly depending not only on SOC but also on battery temperature.

Regarding the temperature dependence of the resistance of each elementary process in an LIB, the following Arrhenius equation usually holds [5,21,43,50]:
(17)
1
R

=
A

where
R

is the resistance of each elementary process,
V

is the activation energy of each elementary process per lithium-ion particle expressed in electron volts, T is the battery temperature,
A

is the pre-exponential factor of each elementary process, e is the charge of an electron, and
k B
is the Boltzmann constant. Taking this into consideration, the Arrhenius plots of the diffusion resistances of the higher and lower frequency side finite length Warburg impedances (
R
d h
and
R
d l
) at SOCs of 80 %, 50 %, and 20 % are shown in Fig. 20 . It can be seen that all the Arrhenius plots are linear, reconfirming the Arrhenius-type temperature dependence for both resistances,
R
d h
and
R
d l
. Eq. (17) shows that a one-to-one relationship exists between the activation energy and the inclination of the corresponding Arrhenius plot. Using this relation, the activation energy of each diffusion resistance was identified from the corresponding linear relationship indicated in Fig. 20. The obtained activation energies of
R
d h
were 0.14, 0.14, and 0.13 eV at SOCs of 80 %, 50 %, and 20 %, respectively, and those of
R
d l
were 0.36, 0.30, and 0.31 eV at SOCs of 80 %, 50 %, and 20 %, respectively, for the test battery. It can be concluded that each activation energy does not greatly depend on SOC and is mainly determined by whether the diffusion resistance is
R
d h
or
R
d l
.

A TDM technique using a preset ECM consisting of many series-connected RC parallel elements was examined as a measurement method of the electrochemical impedance spectrum of an LIB excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change in the low frequency range from several tens of microhertz to several tens of millihertz.

A cylindrical 18,650 type 2.0 Ah LIB with a lithium nickel-manganese-cobalt oxide cathode and graphite anode was selected as the test battery. A comprehensive experimental study was carried out to determine the standard condition of the applied signal suitable for this TDM technique. It was established that an impedance spectrum from 20 μHz to 20 mHz can be accurately measured by selecting a proper, small rate and long duration CC charge or discharge as the applied signal. A CC discharge with a magnitude of 10 mA and duration of 4 h was appropriate for the test battery.

Next, impedance spectra excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change were measured under various SOC and battery temperature conditions by this TDM technique using a preset ECM, and basic characteristics of impedances of the solid state diffusion processes of lithium that exist in the corresponding low frequency range were investigated. It was revealed that the impedance spectrum of an LIB excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change in the low frequency range, from several tens of microhertz to several tens of millihertz, can be reasonably separated into two finite length Warburg impedances that can be clearly characterized by differences in their diffusion time constant values and in the SOC dependence of their diffusion resistances. The time constants of the two finite length Warburg impedances did not change greatly depending on not only SOC but also battery temperature. An Arrhenius-type temperature dependence was confirmed for both of their diffusion resistances. Each of their activation energies was clarified to not greatly depend on SOC and be mainly determined by whether the diffusion resistance was on the higher or lower frequency side. These revelations are due to the fundamental excellence and the appropriate application of the employed TDM technique using a preset ECM which can measure the impedance spectrum while excluding the apparent impedances from OCV change.

Yoshitaka Inui: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Satoshi Hirayama: Software, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Tadashi Tanaka: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization.
